1.1: A theme: Paul opens by defining himself as doulos — slave — of Christ Jesus before naming his apostolic office. The sequence matters: total ownership by Christ precedes and grounds the commission. The trajectory toward Christ shapes even the form of apostolic authority; Paul participates in a mission that belongs to Christ before it belongs to him.
1.2: A direct declaration: the gospel was "promised beforehand through his prophets in the Holy Scriptures." Paul frames the entire letter by insisting that what has occurred in Christ is not an interruption of Israel's covenant story but its fulfillment. The OT is not background material but the originating promise that the gospel brings to completion.
1.3: A fulfillment: "a descendant of David" grounds Christ's identity in the Davidic covenant of 2 Sam 7:12-13, where God promised a son of David whose kingdom would endure forever. Paul's opening verses establish the incarnation not as a generic divine entry into history but as the arrival of the promised Davidic heir — flesh and genealogy are theological, not merely biographical.
1.4: A direct declaration: the resurrection is the moment when Christ is "appointed the Son of God in power." The language echoes Ps 2:7 (the divine decree to the enthroned king: "You are my Son") and Ps 110:1 (the Lord's invitation to the Davidic king to sit at his right hand); Acts 13:33 and Heb 1:5 make the Ps 2 connection explicit. The resurrection is Christ's public enthronement — the moment when his identity as Son is confirmed before the world.
1.5: A fulfillment: the apostolic mission to bring "all the Gentiles to the obedience that comes from faith" is the realized form of the Isaianic Servant's commission to be "a light to the nations" (Isa 49:6). Paul understands his own vocation as participation in Christ's Servant-mission; the global scope of the gospel is not an expansion beyond Israel's categories but the arrival of what the prophets announced.
1.6: A theme: "called to belong to Jesus Christ" — the Roman believers' identity is defined by belonging to a person. The calling-language (κλητοί, klētoi) echoes Israel's election — God's people are defined by divine initiative, not self-selection. In Christ, the category of God's elect people is extended to Gentiles in Rome without abandoning its covenantal shape.
1.7: A revelation of God: "grace and peace from God our Father and from the Lord Jesus Christ" — the paired greeting places the Father and the Son as joint source of grace and peace, a Christological claim embedded in what reads as a conventional greeting. The peace Paul announces is not social or political but the eschatological shalom of the new covenant, now available through Christ.
1.8: A revelation of God: the worldwide report of the Roman church's faith is itself a sign of what the gospel has accomplished — the power of God reaching the imperial capital and producing a community of faith whose existence is itself public testimony. Christ's name is being declared wherever their faith is spoken of.
1.9: A revelation of God: "God, whom I serve in my spirit in preaching the gospel of his Son" — Paul's worship is defined not by temple sacrifice but by gospel proclamation. The Servant of God's Son offers his service through speech, not blood. This represents the temple-worship pattern transposed into the apostolic mission Christ has inaugurated.
1.10: A revelation of God: Paul's prayer for an open door to Rome reflects the understanding that apostolic movement is subject to divine governance. The mission to the nations that Christ commissioned (Matt 28:19; Acts 1:8) proceeds under the Father's timing — Paul plans, God opens or closes the way.
1.11: A revelation of God: "some spiritual gift to make you strong" — the strengthening Paul intends to impart is not a natural resource but a pneumatic one, flowing from the Spirit of Christ. The apostle functions as a conduit for the risen Christ's ongoing ministry of building up his people.
1.12: A revelation of God: mutual encouragement through shared faith is itself a Christological reality — it is "each other's faith" that encourages, not merely mutual goodwill. The faith that unites the community is faith in Christ; its encouragement is therefore an extension of what Christ produces in his people.
1.13: A theme: Paul's mission strategy — seeking "a harvest" among the Gentiles — frames his apostolic work in the harvest-of-the-nations imagery that runs from Isa 27:12 through the prophets and into Jesus' own teaching (Matt 9:37-38; John 4:35-38). The Gentile mission is not Paul's initiative but the completion of a trajectory the prophets anticipated.
1.14: A theme: "obligated both to Greeks and non-Greeks" — the obligation Paul names is not social but missional. Christ's death for all creates a debt of proclamation: the apostle owes the gospel to every people group precisely because the one who died did so for all. The universality of the atonement generates the universality of the mission.
1.15: A revelation of God: Paul's eagerness to preach in Rome — the heart of imperial power — reflects the Christ-shaped inversion of honor and shame. He is "not ashamed" (v16) to bring the gospel to the city where crucifixion-criminals are mocked; what Rome considers weakness, Paul declares to be the power of God.
1.16: A direct declaration: "the gospel is the power of God that brings salvation to everyone who believes: first to the Jew, then to the Gentile." Paul's thesis identifies the gospel not as information about Christ but as the actual vehicle of divine saving power — dynamis theou (power of God) is not metaphorical. The priority order (Jew first) reflects the historical-redemptive sequence: Christ came as Israel's Messiah; the Gentile mission flows from that.
1.17: A direct declaration: "the righteousness of God is revealed — a righteousness that is by faith from first to last." Paul cites Hab 2:4 ("the righteous will live by faith") as the scriptural key: what the prophet announced as the principle of covenant survival through the Babylonian crisis is now disclosed as the universal structure of life before God, fulfilled in Christ. The entire letter argues from this thesis.
1.18: A revelation of God: "the wrath of God is being revealed from heaven against all the godlessness and wickedness of people." The backdrop for the gospel of righteousness (v17) is the revelation of wrath (v18) — both are present-tense revelations. The gospel is not good news abstracted from a problem; it is rescue from a condition Paul now proceeds to describe. Christ's atoning work (3:25) will be the answer to the wrath established here.
1.19: A revelation of God: natural revelation — "what may be known about God is plain to them, because God has made it plain to them." The witness of creation establishes universal accountability; no one stands outside the reach of God's self-disclosure. This universal culpability is the negative premise that makes Christ's rescue universally necessary.
1.20: A revelation of God: "his eternal power and divine nature have been clearly seen, being understood from what has been made, so that people are without excuse." Creation's witness is sufficient to establish guilt — it cannot, Paul will argue, produce righteousness. The law of nature condemns; only the gospel justifies (3:21-26). The same God who made the world now discloses himself definitively in Christ.
1.21: A revelation of God: "although they knew God, they neither glorified him as God nor gave thanks to him." The failure is not ignorance but refusal — a moral suppression rather than an epistemic gap. This root-condition (ungratitude and failure to glorify) is what the gospel reverses: in Christ, God is properly glorified in his people (Rom 15:9; 1 Cor 10:31).
1.22: A revelation of God: "although they claimed to be wise, they became fools." The wisdom-folly inversion here anticipates the logic of 1 Cor 1:18-25, where the cross — itself an apparent folly — is declared the wisdom of God. The world's wisdom that rejects the knowledge of God is the very foolishness that the gospel exposes.
1.23: A shadow: the exchange of "the glory of the immortal God for images" is the anti-type of the divine purpose in creation and salvation. Where idolatry degrades, the gospel restores: Paul will later speak of believers being "transformed into his image" (2 Cor 3:18) and of the glory of God being fully manifest in Christ (2 Cor 4:6). The exchange described here is what redemption reverses.
1.24: A revelation of God: "God gave them over in the sinful desires of their hearts" — the triple paredōken ("gave them over," vv24, 26, 28) presents God's wrath not as arbitrary punishment but as the judicial form of allowing creation to follow its own rebellion to its consequences. The rescue from this condition is what Paul will identify in 5:8: "God demonstrates his own love for us in this: while we were still sinners, Christ died for us."
1.25: A revelation of God: "they worshiped and served created things rather than the Creator." The Creator–creature distinction, fundamental to OT theology and to Israel's covenant, is what all idolatry violates. Christ, whom Paul has just identified as the one through whom creation was ordered (by implication in the Davidic Son language of 1:3-4), is the one who re-establishes right relationship between Creator and creature.
1.26: A revelation of God: "God gave them over to shameful lusts" — the second judicial handing-over reveals how moral disorder proliferates when the fundamental relationship to the Creator is broken. The disorder described is not merely a social problem but a symptom of the theological rupture at humanity's foundation, the rupture that only the gospel addresses.
1.27: A revelation of God: men "received in themselves the due penalty for their error" — the self-destructive consequence of departing from God's design is built into the disordering itself. Paul's moral argument is not moralistic; it is diagnostic. The purpose is not condemnation but to establish that the human condition requires rescue from outside, which the gospel provides.
1.28: A revelation of God: "God gave them over to a depraved mind" — the third handing-over culminates in cognitive corruption. The mind that refuses to retain God becomes functionally disordered. This is the condition that Paul, in later letters, describes Christ's renewal reversing: "be transformed by the renewing of your mind" (12:2). The Christological answer to 1:28 is 12:2.
1.29: A revelation of God: the vice list beginning here is not rhetorical padding but evidence. Paul's argument requires that universal human sinfulness be demonstrated concretely; the list shows that the abstract claim of v18 ("all the godlessness and wickedness of people") has recognizable, specific contents. The universality of this condition is precisely what makes Christ's rescue necessary for everyone.
1.30: A revelation of God: "God-haters, insolent, arrogant and boastful" — the social vices in the list trace back to the theological root of 1:21: failure to glorify God produces inversion in every human relationship. The gospel produces the opposite pattern: humility, truth, love — the character of Christ reproduced in those who are united to him.
1.31: A revelation of God: "no understanding, no fidelity, no love, no mercy" — the four absences describe the collapse of the covenant virtues (faithfulness, steadfast love, mercy) that characterize God himself (Exod 34:6-7) and that he designed his image-bearers to reflect. Christ is the one who displays these perfectly and restores them in those who are in him (Col 3:12-14).
1.32: A revelation of God: "they not only continue to do these very things but also approve of those who practice them." The communal approval of what God's decree condemns is the deepest form of suppression described in 1:18-32 — not merely individual failure but a culture of consent to what God has declared deadly. This communal solidarity in sin is answered by a communal solidarity in grace: "in Christ" as the new corporate identity Paul will develop.

2.1: A revelation of God: "you who pass judgment on someone else, for at whatever point you judge another, you are condemning yourself." The diatribe turns from the pagan to the moralist — whether Gentile philosopher or Torah-observant Jew. The logic is inexorable: one who judges by the same moral standard one violates provides the criteria for one's own condemnation. No one stands outside the need for the righteousness Christ provides.
2.2: A revelation of God: "God's judgment against those who do such things is based on truth." The standard is not social convention or self-assessed virtue but the objective truth of who God is and what he requires. This grounding of judgment in divine truth is what makes the gospel of imputed righteousness the only solution — only a righteousness grounded in God's own truth can meet this standard.
2.3: A revelation of God: "do you think you will escape God's judgment?" — rhetorical question to the moralizing judge who applies standards he himself fails. The assumption of exemption — whether through religious identity, moral effort, or ritual observance — is demolished here as preparation for the announcement that the only escape is through Christ (5:1-2: "justified through faith, we have peace with God").
2.4: A revelation of God: "God's kindness is intended to lead you to repentance." The patience of God is not indifference to sin but a summons — the classical OT characterization of God as slow to anger (Exod 34:6; Joel 2:13) is here given a teleological shape: divine forbearance aims at repentance. Paul will later argue that it is Christ's death that absorbs the wrath which patience deferred (3:25-26).
2.5: A revelation of God: "the day of God's wrath, when his righteous judgment will be revealed" — the eschatological day of judgment that the prophets announced (Zeph 1:14-18) is here established as certain. Paul's letter moves between the present revelation of wrath (1:18) and its future eschatological completion; Christ's intercession (8:34) is what stands between the believer and that day.
2.6: A revelation of God: "God will repay each person according to what they have done" — citing Ps 62:12 and Prov 24:12. Individual moral accountability before a perfectly just God is an axiom Paul affirms, not softens. The gospel does not abolish this principle but fulfills it: Christ bears what the repaid verdict requires for those united to him, so that the "repayment" of wrath falls on him rather than on them.
2.7: A theme: "to those who by persistence in doing good seek glory, honor and immortality, he will give eternal life." The category of those who actually do good consistently enough to inherit eternal life by that route is, Paul will demonstrate (3:10-12), empty. But the category itself is genuine: the standard is real. Christ is the only one who actually meets it, and by faith, those in him receive the verdict they could not earn.
2.8: A revelation of God: wrath and anger for those who "reject the truth and follow evil" — the judicial response to persistent truth-rejection is not arbitrary but proportionate to the gravity of suppressing what God has made plain (1:19-20). The revelation of wrath (1:18) is now given its concrete judicial content: future reckoning for sustained defection from truth.
2.9: A revelation of God: "trouble and distress for every human being who does evil: first for the Jew, then for the Gentile." The priority order of 1:16 ("first to the Jew") applies equally to judgment: the covenant privilege that makes Israel's blessing first also makes Israel's accountability first. This is not anti-Jewish but covenantal — greater privilege entails greater accountability (Amos 3:2).
2.10: A revelation of God: "glory, honor and peace for everyone who does good: first for the Jew, then for the Gentile." The symmetry with v9 reinforces Paul's point about God's impartiality: both the blessing and the curse of the Deuteronomic covenant apply universally. The ground of the promised "peace" (shalom) that Paul will announce in 5:1 ("we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ") is established here as something God gives — not something humanity produces.
2.11: A revelation of God: "God does not show favoritism" — prosōpolēmpsia (partiality, literally "receiving of faces"). This principle, drawn from Deut 10:17, is foundational for Paul's argument that the gospel is for all without distinction (3:22-23). The impartial God who judges impartially also justifies impartially — "to all who believe" (3:22), regardless of ethnic or religious identity.
2.12: A revelation of God: those without the law perish apart from the law; those under it are judged by it. The universality of accountability is preserved across both tracks. This sets up the devastating point Paul is building to: no track — Gentile conscience or Jewish Torah — actually produces the righteousness God requires. Both tracks lead to the same conclusion: the need for what 3:21-24 provides.
2.13: A revelation of God: "it is not those who hear the law who are righteous in God's sight, but it is those who obey the law who will be declared righteous." The standard is genuine obedience, not mere possession or knowledge. Paul will shortly demonstrate (3:9-18) that no one meets this standard — establishing that the "declaration of righteousness" must come from somewhere other than law-observance. Christ is the one who fully obeys and whose obedience is reckoned to faith (5:19).
2.14: A revelation of God: Gentiles who "do by nature things required by the law" demonstrate that the moral structure of God's law is not foreign to humanity but inscribed in it — the image of God (Gen 1:26-27) is not wholly erased. This partial witness anticipates the new covenant's full work: writing the law on the heart by the Spirit (Jer 31:33; Ezek 36:26-27), which Paul will later describe as fulfilled in those who walk by the Spirit (8:4).
2.15: A shadow: the law "written on their hearts" in conscience is a partial and unreliable anticipation of the new covenant promise of Jer 31:33, where God himself writes his law on the heart fully and effectively. Paul's language here deliberately echoes Jeremiah's new covenant passage; the conscience is the shadow, the Spirit's work in the new covenant is the substance. The shadow establishes accountability; the substance is what Christ's work makes possible.
2.16: A direct declaration: "the day when God judges people's secrets through Jesus Christ." The eschatological judgment — already established in vv5-6 — is here identified as mediated through Christ. The one who died for sinners is also the one who will judge them; the gospel and the judgment have the same Christ at their center. This is the only explicit mention of Christ in this chapter, placed at its structural climax: all of Paul's argument about sin and judgment converges on the one through whom God will adjudicate.
2.17: A revelation of God: the diatribe now addresses directly the Jew who relies on the law and boasts in God. Paul is not critiquing Jewish identity as such but the assumption that covenant privilege functions as a shield against the universal accountability he has established. The critique is the prophetic tradition's own (Mic 3:11; Jer 7:4): claiming God without corresponding obedience.
2.18: A revelation of God: "you know his will and approve of what is superior because you are instructed by the law." The law's genuine advantage — giving knowledge of God's will — is acknowledged, not denied. But knowledge of the superior without corresponding practice deepens rather than reduces culpability. Paul will argue in ch7 that the law cannot produce what it reveals to be necessary.
2.19: A revelation of God: "you are convinced that you are a guide for the blind, a light for those who are in the dark." Paul echoes the Isaianic Servant language (Isa 42:6-7) applied now to the Jewish teacher's self-understanding. The irony is severe: Israel was given the Servant's mission-language, but the Servant himself has now come and been rejected. The teachers of the blind are themselves blind (John 9:39-41).
2.20: A revelation of God: "instructor of the foolish, a teacher of little children, because you have in the law the embodiment of knowledge and truth." The law is genuinely the embodiment of knowledge and truth — Paul does not dispute this. But the one who possesses the embodiment while failing its demands is in a worse position than the one who never had it. Privilege is responsibility.
2.21: A revelation of God: "you, then, who teach others, do you not teach yourself?" The rhetorical questions are from the prophetic tradition (Ps 50:16-21; Mic 3:11) — the gap between claimed identity and actual conduct is Israel's persistent failure, which the prophets indicted across centuries. Paul reprises the indictment not to condemn Israel uniquely but to establish that no ethnic or religious category provides exemption from the universal need for grace.
2.22: A revelation of God: "you who say people should not commit adultery, do you commit adultery?" The questions intensify: stealing, adultery, robbing temples. The specific transgressions suggest Paul may be drawing on known patterns rather than rhetorical invention. The point is structural: moral knowledge without moral power is not righteousness but heightened condemnation. What is needed is not better instruction but a new nature — what Christ accomplishes through the Spirit.
2.23: A revelation of God: "you who boast in the law, do you dishonor God by breaking the law?" Boasting in the law while breaking it is the precise form of self-deception Paul dismantles. The solution is not better law-keeping but the righteousness that comes through faith (3:22) — a righteousness external to the self, received rather than achieved, which is what the boasting of 3:27 will be excluded from claiming.
2.24: A revelation of God: Paul quotes Isa 52:5 LXX — "God's name is blasphemed among the Gentiles because of you." In Isaiah the charge falls on Israel in exile; Paul applies it to Torah-breaking Jews in the diaspora. The name of God, which Israel was called to make known among the nations, is dishonored by the conduct of those who bear it. Christ, in contrast, is the one through whom God's name is rightly glorified among the Gentiles (Rom 15:9).
2.25: A theme: "circumcision has value if you observe the law" — Paul engages the foundational covenant sign of Gen 17 not to abolish it but to expose the conditionality built into its own terms. Gen 17:14 specifies excision for the uncircumcised who breaks the covenant; circumcision without covenant-keeping is self-undermining. This argument prepares for the redefinition Paul will offer in vv28-29 and develop fully in ch4 (Abraham's faith before circumcision).
2.26: A revelation of God: "if those who are not circumcised keep the law's requirements, will they not be regarded as though they were circumcised?" The hypothetical raises the question of what circumcision actually signifies. Paul's answer will be Spirit-worked inner transformation (v29), which is the new covenant fulfillment of what the external sign always pointed toward. The uncircumcised Gentile who obeys by the Spirit exceeds the circumcised Jew who violates by the flesh.
2.27: A revelation of God: "the one who is not circumcised physically and yet obeys the law will condemn you who, even though you have the written code and circumcision, are a lawbreaker." The possession of the external sign without the internal reality inverts the anticipated order of privilege and judgment. Paul's logic here anticipates the new covenant community: what matters is not the sign but the reality the sign was designed to represent.
2.28: A revelation of God: "a person is not a Jew who is one only outwardly." Paul does not abolish Jewish identity but redefines its essence. The external markers — circumcision, law, ethnic descent — are not the core; they are the outer form of an inner reality. This prepares the radical claim of v29 and the redefinition of "Israel" that Paul will return to in chs 9-11.
2.29: A fulfillment: "circumcision is circumcision of the heart, by the Spirit, not by the written code." Paul explicitly fulfills the new covenant promise of Jer 31:33 ("I will put my law in their minds and write it on their hearts") and the Deuteronomic demand of Deut 30:6 ("the Lord your God will circumcise your hearts"). What Moses foresaw and Jeremiah promised, the Spirit of Christ effects in those who believe. The "true Jew" is defined by the Spirit's work — the eschatological fulfillment has arrived.

3.1: A revelation of God: "what advantage, then, is there in being a Jew?" Paul's diatribal partner raises the obvious objection: if moral failure is universal and external circumcision ultimately inadequate, why does Israel's election matter? Paul will not dismiss the question — Jewish privilege is real (v2), and the entire structure of redemptive history runs through Israel's covenant with God, culminating in Israel's Messiah.
3.2: A revelation of God: "the Jews have been entrusted with the very words of God." The advantage is specific and historical: Israel was the custodian of the divine oracles — the Torah, the prophets, the promises. This custodianship was not ethnic superiority but covenant stewardship. The oracles they held contained the gospel Paul now preaches (1:2: "promised beforehand through his prophets in the Holy Scriptures").
3.3: A revelation of God: "will their unfaithfulness nullify God's faithfulness?" The question is whether Israel's covenant failure can undo the divine commitment. Paul's answer will be a consistent no — divine faithfulness does not depend on human faithfulness. This principle is the ground of the gospel: God's righteousness is revealed "apart from the law" (3:21) precisely because human law-keeping has failed as a vehicle of covenant faithfulness.
3.4: A revelation of God: "let God be true, and every human being a liar." Paul cites Ps 51:4 — David's penitential confession that God is vindicated in his judgment. The universal acknowledgment of human guilt and divine truthfulness is the baseline reality Scripture itself establishes. The gospel does not create this condition; it answers it: Christ is the one who takes the verdict ("just") and makes the sinner "justified" (3:26).
3.5: A revelation of God: "if our unrighteousness brings out God's righteousness more clearly, what shall we say?" The diatribal objector floats the antinomian conclusion: if sin showcases divine righteousness, is God unjust to punish it? Paul labels this a "human argument" — it reasons from the effect (divine glory revealed through sin) to a conclusion (sin is therefore acceptable) that confuses God's capacity to bring good from evil with divine approval of evil. Christ's death, not ongoing sin, is what demonstrates God's righteousness (3:25-26).
3.6: A revelation of God: "certainly not! If that were so, how could God judge the world?" The right of God to judge the world — assumed throughout Scripture from Gen 18:25 onward — would be incoherent if sin were somehow divinely sanctioned. Paul's brief response preserves both divine righteousness and divine judgment as foundations that the gospel addresses rather than dissolves.
3.7: A revelation of God: the hypothetical objection intensifies — "if my falsehood enhances God's truthfulness... why am I still condemned as a sinner?" The argument exploits the genuine logic that God's glory is displayed through contrast with human failure. Paul's answer, given implicitly by the rhetorical force of v8, is that the logic collapses under its own weight: a God who could not condemn what increases his glory would be neither truthful nor just.
3.8: A revelation of God: "why not say — let us do evil that good may result?" Paul names the antinomian slander as a known misrepresentation of his gospel. The charge that grace-teaching leads to moral license is not a later misreading but a first-century accusation. Paul's answer here is brief ("their condemnation is just"); his full answer is Romans 6, where union with Christ's death and resurrection is shown to be the death of sin's dominion, not its license.
3.9: A revelation of God: "Jews and Gentiles alike are all under the power of sin." The Greek hyph' hamartian ("under sin") is a power-language — sin is not merely a category of acts but a regime. This sets up the Christological resolution of ch5-8: what liberates from sin's dominion is not moral effort or covenant status but union with Christ, who has entered and broken sin's realm through his death (6:10: "the death he died, he died to sin once for all").
3.10: A revelation of God: "there is no one righteous, not even one" — Paul opens his OT catena with Ps 14:1-3 (cf. Ps 53:1-3). The psalm surveys humanity from God's perspective and returns the verdict of universal corruption. Paul wields it not as rhetoric but as Scripture's own testimony, closing off any Jewish exemption: the psalms are Israel's Scripture, and Israel's Scripture declares Israel equally under indictment.
3.11: A revelation of God: "there is no one who understands; there is no one who seeks God." The continuation of the Ps 14 catena establishes that the failure is not merely moral but cognitive and volitional: understanding and seeking God — the two foundational religious capacities — are absent. This is the condition of humanity apart from divine initiative. The gospel is precisely that God sought those who were not seeking him (Rom 10:20, quoting Isa 65:1).
3.12: A revelation of God: "all have turned away, they have together become worthless; there is no one who does good, not even one." The third line of the catena extends the verdict: defection is universal ("all"), social ("together"), and total ("not even one" exception). Paul is not arguing for misanthropy but for the necessity of what 3:21-24 provides: a righteousness that comes entirely from outside the human moral record.
3.13: A revelation of God: Paul adds Ps 5:9 ("their throats are open graves; their tongues practice deceit") and Ps 140:3 ("the poison of vipers is on their lips"). The organs of speech become the site of the indictment — what comes out of the human mouth reveals the condition of the heart. Jesus will make the same point (Matt 15:18-19). The universality of speech-corruption closes off the route of self-defense through verbal protest.
3.14: A revelation of God: Ps 10:7 — "their mouths are full of cursing and bitterness." The catena continues its anatomy of human corruption through speech: from open graves (v13) to cursing and bitterness (v14). Paul's accumulation is structural, not exhaustive; he selects texts that cover the full range of human faculties — throat, tongue, lips, mouth — to show the comprehensiveness of sin's reach.
3.15: A revelation of God: Isa 59:7-8 enters the catena — "their feet are swift to shed blood." Isaiah's indictment of Israel's corporate moral failure is now deployed by Paul as evidence not against Israel specifically but against humanity universally. The prophets who accused Israel are the same prophets whose words establish the universal verdict. Paul's catena thus uses Israel's own Scriptures to close every avenue of covenant-exemption.
3.16: A revelation of God: continuing Isa 59:8 — "ruin and misery mark their paths." The consequences of the moral corruption catalogued in the catena are social destruction: ruin (syntrimma, brokenness) and misery. This is not merely individual but structural — the human project of building civilization is undermined by the condition sin produces. The gospel reverses this at its root, not merely at the level of improved behavior.
3.17: A revelation of God and a theme: "the way of peace they do not know." The catena reaches its penultimate indictment with the absence of peace. The peace (shalom) that Israel's prophets promised as the mark of the coming kingdom (Isa 9:6; 52:7) is precisely what fallen humanity cannot produce or find. Paul will announce in 5:1 that "we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ" — the answer to the "way of peace they do not know."
3.18: A revelation of God: Ps 36:1 closes the catena — "there is no fear of God before their eyes." The psalmist identifies the root beneath all the corruptions catalogued: the absence of the fear of God. Paul places this at the end as the theological summary of the preceding seven quotations. Every specific failure (speech, violence, social destruction) flows from this foundational defection. The beginning of wisdom — and the beginning of the gospel's reversal — is here.
3.19: A revelation of God: "so that every mouth may be silenced and the whole world held accountable to God." The purpose of the law's indictment is universal accountability — not the conviction of a subset (Gentiles, or irreligious Jews) but every mouth, including the most observant. The courtroom imagery (silenced mouth, accountable world) establishes the forensic frame within which 3:21-26 will announce the verdict-reversal that the gospel provides.
3.20: A revelation of God: "no one will be declared righteous in God's sight by the works of the law; rather, through the law we become conscious of our sin." The law's function is diagnostic, not curative — it reveals the disease without providing the remedy. Paul draws on Ps 143:2 ("no living person is righteous before you") and stands in tension with Lev 18:5 (life through doing): the life-through-doing route is foreclosed because no one actually does it fully. This verse is the negative premise that makes 3:21 ("but now") the hinge of the letter.
3.21: A direct declaration: "but now apart from the law the righteousness of God has been made known." Nyni de ("but now") is the most decisive temporal hinge in Romans — the shift from the long diagnosis of condemnation (1:18–3:20) to the announcement of rescue. The righteousness of God is not new — "the Law and the Prophets testify" to it (Isa 53:11; Isa 51:5-6) — but it has now been definitively disclosed and enacted in Christ's death and resurrection.
3.22: A direct declaration: "this righteousness is given through faith in Jesus Christ to all who believe. There is no difference between Jew and Gentile." The gospel's two revolutionary claims: the vehicle is faith in Christ (not law-observance), and the scope is universal (no distinction). The two claims are logically connected: because righteousness comes through Christ and not through the ethnic-covenantal marker of the law, it is available to all who trust him.
3.23: A revelation of God: "for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God." The "glory of God" fallen humanity fails to reach is the divine presence and honor that image-bearers were created to reflect (Gen 1:26-27) and that the Fall forfeited (Gen 3:23-24). The restoration of that glory is what the gospel accomplishes: "the glory of God in the face of Christ" (2 Cor 4:6); believers are being "transformed into his image with ever-increasing glory" (2 Cor 3:18).
3.24: A direct declaration: "all are justified freely by his grace through the redemption that came by Christ Jesus." Three interlocking gifts: justification (the verdict of righteousness), grace (the basis — unmerited), and redemption (the mechanism — the liberation Christ accomplished). The term apolytrōsis (redemption) carries Exodus overtones (Exod 6:6) — Christ's work is the definitive new-exodus rescue from sin's bondage, the event all prior redemptions anticipated.
3.25: A direct fulfillment: "God presented Christ as a hilastērion — a sacrifice of atonement through the shedding of his blood." The term hilastērion is precisely the LXX word for the kapporeth (the mercy seat, the golden cover of the ark) where atonement blood was applied on the Day of Atonement (Lev 16:14-16; Exod 25:17-22). Paul presents Christ as the fulfillment of this type: the locus where God and humanity meet, where blood is presented, where sin is covered. The structural correspondence (atoning blood, divine presence, covering of previously unpunished sin) is exact and deliberate.
3.26: A direct declaration: "so as to be just and the one who justifies those who have faith in Jesus." The apparent paradox of divine justice — how can the righteous God declare guilty sinners righteous? — is resolved at the cross. The hilastērion (v25) absorbs the wrath that justice required; the guilty are justified without injustice because judgment has fallen. Paul's climactic claim is that the gospel simultaneously upholds God's justice and extends his justifying mercy — a resolution Isa 45:21 anticipated but could not yet explain.
3.27: A revelation of God: "where, then, is boasting? It is excluded." The gospel ends all grounds for human self-commendation before God. The "law of faith" (the principle that righteousness is received, not achieved) structures out the possibility of claiming credit. The appropriate response to the gospel is not pride but gratitude — the doxological character of the letter's later chapters (11:33-36; 15:5-13) follows directly from this exclusion.
3.28: A direct declaration: "a person is justified by faith apart from the works of the law." This is the letter's thesis distilled to its simplest form. Paul will spend chs4-5 arguing from Abraham and Adam; chs6-8 addressing the moral implications; chs9-11 addressing Israel's place in the plan. All of it unfolds from this declaration: justification is by faith in Christ, not by law-performance.
3.29: A revelation of God: "or is God the God of Jews only? Is he not the God of Gentiles too?" The monotheism of the Shema (Deut 6:4) implies the universal scope of God's justifying work. One God means one standard of access — faith — for all peoples. The gospel is not a departure from Israel's theology but its logical extension: if God is truly the God of all creation, then his righteousness reaches all who believe, from every nation.
3.30: A direct declaration: "there is only one God, who will justify the circumcised by faith and the uncircumcised through that same faith." Monotheism generates the unity of the justifying method: one God, one means (faith), two covenant tracks (circumcised and uncircumcised) now unified in Christ. The Jew-Gentile distinction that structured redemptive history is not abolished but fulfilled — absorbed into the one people of God constituted by faith in Israel's Messiah.
3.31: A revelation of God: "do we, then, nullify the law by this faith? Not at all! Rather, we uphold the law." The accusation that grace-faith destroys Torah is answered in two ways here: (1) the law itself testified to this righteousness (3:21), so faith vindicates the law's witness rather than undermining it; (2) in ch4, Paul will show that Abraham's justification by faith preceded and was confirmed by his circumcision. The law's deepest intention — to bring people into right relationship with God — is fulfilled by faith in Christ, not annulled.

4.1: A theme: Paul begins the Abraham chapter with "What shall we say that Abraham, our forefather according to the flesh, discovered?" The question frames Abraham as a test case for the principle just announced (3:28). Abraham is the OT exemplar of the very faith-righteousness that the gospel declares; his story is not background color but the first-covenant proof of the Christ-principle.
4.2: A theme: "If, in fact, Abraham was justified by works, he had something to boast about — but not before God." The exclusion of boasting before God is the negative condition that reveals the structure of justification: it must be received, not earned, which is why Christ's righteousness rather than human achievement is the ground.
4.3: A direct: "Abraham believed God, and it was credited to him as righteousness" (Gen 15:6). This is the Scripture Paul presents as the backbone of his argument. The faith that was reckoned as righteousness anticipated the faith-in-the-risen-Christ of 4:24 — Abraham's trust in the God who calls the dead to life is the same structural act as trusting in the one who raised Jesus from the dead.
4.4: A theme: "Now to the one who works, wages are not credited as a gift but as an obligation." The wages-versus-gift contrast exposes the logic of all self-achievement religion: it produces debt-credits, not grace. Christ's righteousness given freely (3:24, dōrean) is structurally incapable of arising from the works-and-wages framework.
4.5: A direct: "However, to the one who does not work but trusts God who justifies the ungodly, their faith is credited as righteousness." The phrase "God who justifies the ungodly" is the theological center of Paul's Christology in miniature: God does not justify the qualified but the ungodly, which required the atoning work of 3:24-26 to make possible. This verse states the gospel's scandalous logic.
4.6: A type: David's blessing of the person whose sin is covered and to whom God does not credit iniquity (Ps 32:1-2) is another OT witness to the same principle as Abraham's reckoning. The person blessed in Ps 32 has no merit of their own — the blessing is the covering of sin. This is the type that Christ's atoning work fulfills.
4.7: A type: "Blessed are those whose transgressions are forgiven, whose sins are covered." The Psalm's blessing rests on divine action (forgiving, covering), not human performance. The gospel announces the ground on which God can legitimately cover sin: Christ bearing what the sinner deserved (3:25-26).
4.8: A type: "Blessed is the one whose sin the Lord will never count against them." The double negative (ou mē logisetai) is emphatic: the Lord will absolutely not credit this person's sin. The basis for this non-counting is left implicit in the Psalm; Paul's argument is that Christ's atoning death is what makes the non-imputation legally warranted rather than mere benign neglect.
4.9: A theme: Paul asks whether this blessedness comes to the circumcised or also to the uncircumcised. The question sets up the argument that Abraham's faith-righteousness preceded his circumcision, establishing the faith-principle as prior to and independent of the covenant-sign. This positions Christ's benefits as available apart from ethnic membership.
4.10: A theme: "It was not after, but before he was circumcised." The temporal sequence is the point: faith-righteousness came first, circumcision second. This temporal precedence is the structural argument for the priority of faith over sign in the order of salvation — which grounds the inclusion of Gentile believers in Abraham's spiritual offspring.
4.11: A type: Abraham received circumcision as a seal of the righteousness he already had through faith, making him the father of all who believe without being circumcised. The seal (circumcision) confirms rather than confers the faith-righteousness. Christ's death and resurrection are similarly confirmatory and constitutive of a righteousness received by faith, not generated by rite.
4.12: A theme: Abraham is father of the circumcised who also walk in his footsteps of faith. The double qualification insists that the sign without the faith is insufficient; the faith without the sign is sufficient. This establishes the principle that Gentile believers are Abraham's children not despite lacking circumcision but because they share his faith-structure.
4.13: A direct: "It was not through the law that Abraham and his offspring received the promise that he would be heir of the world, but through the righteousness that comes by faith." The world-inheritance promise (Ps 2:8; Gen 17:6) is given to Christ as the true seed (Gal 3:16) and to those in him. The "heir of the world" language anticipates the universal scope of Christ's kingdom.
4.14: A theme: "If those who depend on the law are heirs, faith means nothing and the promise is worthless." The argument by reductio establishes that the promise-structure is incompatible with a law-compliance structure; a promise by definition cannot be fulfilled by the recipient's own performance. Christ's fulfillment of the law on behalf of others is what keeps the promise intact.
4.15: A theme: "The law brings wrath" — because where there is law, transgression becomes defined and punishable. The law's function as wrath-generator points toward the need for a solution outside the law; Christ absorbing the wrath the law pronounces (3:25; 8:3) is the only resolution to this dynamic.
4.16: A direct: "Therefore, the promise comes by faith, so that it may be by grace and may be guaranteed to all Abraham's offspring — not only to those who are of the law but also to those who have the faith of Abraham." Grace guarantees what law cannot, because law conditions on human performance while grace rests on divine faithfulness. Christ is the guarantee of the new covenant (Heb 7:22) — the same logic Paul is establishing here.
4.17: A type: Abraham believed in "the God who gives life to the dead and calls into being things that were not." This characterization of God as the resurrection-God and the Creator-God ex nihilo is Paul's preparation for the following verses. Abraham's faith in this God structurally parallels faith in the God who raised Jesus; the resurrection is the defining act of the same God Abraham trusted.
4.18: A type: "Against all hope, Abraham in hope believed and so became the father of many nations." The faith-against-circumstance that characterizes Abraham is the OT pattern for all Christian faith — trusting in divine promise when natural conditions offer no ground for hope. The resurrection of Christ is the supreme case of the God who "gives life to the dead."
4.19: A type: Abraham "faced the fact that his body was as good as dead — since he was about a hundred years old — and that Sarah's womb was also dead." The bodily death that faith must overcome in Abraham (and Sarah) is the shadow of the physical death that Christ's resurrection overcomes; in both cases, new life comes not from biological resources but from divine creative power.
4.20: A type: Abraham "did not waver through unbelief regarding the promise of God, but was strengthened in his faith and gave glory to God." The persevering faith that gives glory to God rather than collapsing before impossible circumstances is the type of faith Paul will describe as the response to the resurrection announcement (v.24). Abraham's faith and resurrection-faith are the same structural act in different historical moments.
4.21: A type: Abraham was "fully persuaded that God had power to do what he had promised." The persuasion is about divine power, not human possibility; the faith attaches to God's capacity, not to visible evidence. This is precisely what Paul will say about resurrection-faith: it is trust in the power of the God who raised Jesus from the dead.
4.22: A direct: "This is why 'it was credited to him as righteousness.'" The repetition of the Gen 15:6 citation from v.3 forms a bracket around the Abraham argument; his faith-righteousness is the model Paul has been expounding, and the model points beyond itself to the fulfillment in Christ.
4.23: A direct: "The words 'it was credited to him' were written not for him alone." Paul's hermeneutical principle: the OT narrative was recorded for the sake of the new covenant community. Abraham's faith and its reckoning as righteousness is not a peculiarity of one man's biography but the disclosure of the principle by which God deals with all who trust him.
4.24: A direct: Faith is also credited to us "who believe in him who raised Jesus our Lord from the dead." The object of faith is now specified as the resurrection of Jesus, and the God who performed it. Abraham's faith and Christian faith share the same God and the same structure (trust in the life-giving God) now applied to the specific event of the resurrection.
4.25: A direct: "He was delivered over to death for our sins and was raised to life for our justification." The two-clause summary of the atonement and resurrection is one of the most compressed Christological statements in the NT. The delivery-for-sins corresponds to the sin-offering logic of 3:25; the raising-for-justification shows that the resurrection is not merely a miracle but the judicial declaration that the atoning sacrifice was accepted.

5.1: A direct: "Therefore, since we have been justified through faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ." The peace is not a feeling but a covenantal status — the end of the enmity (cf. 8:7, the flesh hostile to God) achieved through the mediatorial work of Christ. Peace-with-God is the first-named consequence of the justification Paul has been establishing.
5.2: A direct: "We have gained access by faith into this grace in which we now stand." Prosagōgē (access) is a court-and-temple term: the right to come before the sovereign. Christ provides this access (Eph 2:18; 3:12) where the law could not. Boasting in hope of glory reverses the shame of 3:23 ("falling short of the glory of God") with eschatological confidence.
5.3: A theme: "We also glory in our sufferings, because we know that suffering produces perseverance." The paradox of finding ground for confidence in suffering rather than in achievement is the ethic of those who have been reconciled by Christ's death when they were enemies (v.10). The suffering-to-glory sequence parallels Christ's own path (8:17).
5.4: A theme: Perseverance produces character, and character hope. The virtuous sequence of v.3-4 is the outworking in the believer of what Christ's resurrection established: death does not have the final word; suffering is the seedbed of life. The hope it produces does not disappoint because it rests on the same God who raised Christ.
5.5: A direct: "God's love has been poured out into our hearts through the Holy Spirit, who has been given to us." The Spirit poured out is the fulfillment of Joel 2:28-29 and Ezek 36:26-27; the vehicle of this love is the Spirit given because of Christ's atoning work (cf. Gal 4:4-6). The love poured in is the experiential confirmation of the reconciliation announced in vv.1-2.
5.6: A direct: "You see, at just the right time, when we were still powerless, Christ died for the ungodly." The timing (kairō = the appointed time) grounds the cross in divine sovereign purpose, not contingency. The recipients — "powerless," "ungodly" — define the scandalous direction of Christ's self-giving: not toward the deserving but toward those with no claim whatsoever.
5.7: A direct: "Very rarely will anyone die for a righteous person, though for a good person someone might possibly dare to die." Paul argues by analogy from human experience: even the most heroic human death for the worthy is exceptional. Christ's death for enemies (v.10) and the ungodly (v.6) is of an entirely different category — which is why it reveals and demonstrates the love of God rather than merely human virtue.
5.8: A direct: "But God demonstrates his own love for us in this: while we were still sinners, Christ died for us." The demonstration (synistēsin = commends, proves by proof) is an objective act of God in history, not a feeling. The Christological claim is absolute: Christ's death is God's own self-demonstration of love — identifying Christ's act with God's own action.
5.9: A direct: "Since we have now been justified by his blood, how much more shall we be saved from God's wrath through him!" The a fortiori argument: if the more difficult thing (justification of sinners) has been accomplished, the less difficult (deliverance from wrath at the final judgment) is secure. The blood — the sacrificial offering language — is the basis of present justification and future deliverance.
5.10: A direct: "For if, while we were God's enemies, we were reconciled to him through the death of his Son, how much more, having been reconciled, shall we be saved through his life!" Reconciliation through Christ's death and salvation through his resurrected life are the two phases of the same redemption. The living Christ (not only the dying Christ) is Paul's ground of confidence for final salvation.
5.11: A direct: "We also boast in God through our Lord Jesus Christ, through whom we have now received reconciliation." The mediation is explicit: all access to God, all basis for confident relationship with God, comes through (dia) Jesus Christ. Boasting in God through Christ is the eschatological inversion of boasting in self-achievement.
5.12: A type: "Therefore, just as sin entered the world through one man, and death through sin, and in this way death came to all people, because all sinned..." The Adam-narrative begins. Paul's argument in vv.12-21 establishes Adam as the type whose action determined the condition of all who came after him — by analogy with and contrast to Christ, whose action determines the condition of all in him.
5.13: A theme: "To be sure, sin was in the world before the law was given, but sin is not charged against anyone's account where there is no law." The pre-law period demonstrates that death reigned independently of legal condemnation — which establishes that the death-problem is deeper than legal transgression and requires more than law-keeping to resolve.
5.14: A type: "Nevertheless, death reigned from the time of Adam to the time of Moses, even over those who did not sin by breaking a command, as did Adam, who is a pattern of the one to come." Paul explicitly names Adam as a type (typos) of Christ. The structural correspondence: one man's act determines the condition of all — in Adam, all die; in Christ, all (who are in him) are made alive. This is not a reader-imposed analogy; it is Paul's own typological argument.
5.15: A type: "But the gift is not like the trespass. For if the many died by the trespass of the one man, how much more did God's grace and the gift that came by the grace of the one man, Jesus Christ, overflow to the many!" The gift far exceeds the trespass in both quantity (perisseuō = overflow) and quality (grace versus condemnation). The type-and-antitype relationship is asymmetrical: Christ's act is infinitely more productive than Adam's was destructive.
5.16: A type: "Nor can the gift of God be compared with the result of one man's sin: The judgment followed one sin and brought condemnation, but the gift followed many trespasses and brought justification." The economy of divine grace inverts the economy of sin: one sin brought one condemnation; many sins brought one justification. The multiplication of sin does not outpace the gift.
5.17: A direct: "For if, by the trespass of the one man, death reigned through that one man, how much more will those who receive God's abundant provision of grace and of the gift of righteousness reign in life through the one man, Jesus Christ!" The reign of life through Christ explicitly answers the reign of death through Adam. The two-realm structure (death's reign vs. life's reign) is the Christological frame for the entire passage.
5.18: A direct: "Consequently, just as one trespass resulted in condemnation for all people, so also one righteous act resulted in justification and life for all people." The parallel is exact: one act, universal consequence. The "one righteous act" (dikaiōma) is Christ's atoning obedience, which Paul specifies in the next verse as his "obedience." This is the gospel's claim in its most compressed form.
5.19: A direct: "For just as through the disobedience of the one man the many were made sinners, so also through the obedience of the one man the many will be made righteous." The "obedience of Christ" is the active dimension of his life and death that accomplishes what Adam's disobedience undid. This is the basis of what later theology called Christ's active obedience — not merely dying for sin but living the perfect obedience that constitutes righteousness for all in him.
5.20: A theme: "The law was brought in so that the trespass might increase. But where sin increased, grace increased all the more." The law's role as sin-multiplier (by defining and therefore increasing transgression) is the penultimate word; grace's super-abundance is the final word. The law drives toward the need for grace; grace drives toward Christ who fulfills the law.
5.21: A direct: "So that, just as sin reigned in death, so also grace might reign through righteousness to bring eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord." The final verse of the Adam/Christ argument closes the comparison: grace reigns through righteousness (Christ's own righteousness imputed and imparted), and its destination is eternal life — not death. The mediatorial phrase "through Jesus Christ our Lord" anchors the entire economy of grace in the specific person of the risen Lord.

6.1: A theme: "What shall we say, then? Shall we go on sinning so that grace may increase?" The antinomian objection to grace is addressed. The question shows that Paul's gospel (ch.5 on grace's super-abundance) is radical enough to attract the charge of moral permissiveness — which confirms the depth of grace's unconditionality. His answer (vv.2-14) is grounded entirely in union with Christ, not in moral obligation as such.
6.2: A direct: "By no means! We are those who have died to sin; how can we live in it any longer?" Death-to-sin is not aspirational language but a description of a status change that occurred in union with Christ's death. The rhetorical force is: if you died to sin in Christ, continuing to live in it is ontologically impossible, not merely morally inadvisable.
6.3: A direct: "Or don't you know that all of us who were baptized into Christ Jesus were baptized into his death?" Baptism as union with Christ's death establishes the basis of vv.2 and 4. The death Paul is invoking is not metaphorical: it is participation in the historical death of Jesus, appropriated through faith and symbolized in baptism. The believer's identity is now defined by Christ's death.
6.4: A direct: "We were therefore buried with him through baptism into death in order that, just as Christ was raised from the dead through the glory of the Father, we too may live a new life." The resurrection of Christ is not merely the believer's future hope but the pattern and power of present new life. The logic of ch.4's resurrection-God is applied here: the God who raised Christ works in believers the same new-creation power.
6.5: A direct: "For if we have been united with him in a death like his, we will certainly also be united with him in a resurrection like his." The Greek symphytos (G4854, united with/grown together) is a biological term for organisms that have been grafted together; the union is organic, not merely legal. The certainty of the future resurrection grounds the present death-to-sin.
6.6: A direct: "For we know that our old self was crucified with him so that the body ruled by sin might be done away with, that we should no longer be slaves to sin." The "old self" (palaios anthrōpos) is the Adamic identity; its crucifixion with Christ is the decisive end of the sin-dominion established in ch.5. This is not progressive sanctification but a definitive event accomplished in Christ's death.
6.7: A theme: "Because anyone who has died has been set free from sin." The axiom: death terminates the claims of a prior domain. As a deceased person cannot be claimed by their former employer, so the one who has died with Christ is released from sin's legal hold. The resurrection of Christ confirms that the release from sin's dominion is permanent.
6.8: A direct: "Now if we died with Christ, we believe that we will also live with him." The co-death establishes the co-life; they cannot be separated. The future "will live with him" encompasses both the present spiritual life and the future bodily resurrection, both grounded in the same union with Christ.
6.9: A direct: "For we know that since Christ was raised from the dead, he cannot die again; death no longer has mastery over him." Christ's resurrection is permanent and unrepeatable: death's dominion over him is definitively ended. The resurrection is not resuscitation (return to the previous life) but transformation into a mode of existence no longer subject to death.
6.10: A direct: "The death he died, he died to sin once for all; but the life he lives, he lives to God." The adverb ephapax (once for all, G2178) marks the unrepeatable character of Christ's death to sin; its once-for-all nature is the ground for its universal sufficiency. The life he now lives is wholly oriented toward God — which is the pattern for the believer's own ethical life (v.11).
6.11: A direct: "In the same way, count yourselves dead to sin but alive to God in Christ Jesus." The imperative logizesthe (count/reckon, the imputation-verb from ch.4) is applied here to personal identity: believers are to reckon their death and life according to their union with Christ rather than according to sin's claim. The status is established; the reckoning brings behavior into conformity with reality.
6.12: A theme: "Therefore do not let sin reign in your mortal body so that you obey its evil desires." The exhortation follows from the indicative (vv.6-11); sin's reign has been broken in principle, and the practical realization of that break is the ethical task. "Mortal body" holds the tension: the body is still mortal (not yet resurrected) but need not be sin's instrument.
6.13: A direct: "Do not offer any part of yourself to sin as an instrument of wickedness, but rather offer yourselves to God as those who have been brought from death to life." The phrase "brought from death to life" is resurrection language applied to present existence; the transfer from death-domain to life-domain has occurred (v.4), and the ethical consequence is to present oneself to God in keeping with that transfer.
6.14: A direct: "For sin shall no longer be your master, because you are not under the law, but under grace." The kur- (master) language: sin's dominion is ended not by effort but by a change of covenant domain. Being under grace rather than law means standing in the sphere of Christ's accomplished work; sin's claim has no legal standing in that domain.
6.15: A theme: "What then? Shall we sin because we are not under the law but under grace?" The second antinomian objection is similar to the first (v.1) but focuses on grace's freedom from law rather than grace's super-abundance. The grace Paul preaches is radical enough that misunderstanding it as license is understandable — which is evidence of the gospel's genuine unconditionality.
6.16: A theme: "Don't you know that when you offer yourselves to someone as obedient slaves, you are slaves of the one you obey — whether you are slaves to sin, which leads to death, or to obedience, which leads to righteousness?" The slave-and-master binary replaces the law-and-grace binary of the previous argument; total allegiance to one master is the condition of the human will, and the transfer from sin's ownership to Christ's is the gospel's accomplishment.
6.17: A direct: "Thanks be to God that, though you used to be slaves to sin, you have come to obey from your heart the pattern of teaching that has now claimed your allegiance." The thanks is to God because the transfer of allegiance was God's act, not the believer's. "Pattern of teaching" (typos didachēs) is the apostolic deposit about Christ that reshapes the whole person.
6.18: A direct: "You have been set free from sin and have become slaves to righteousness." The passive eleutherōthentes (you were freed) indicates divine action; the active edoulōthēte (you became enslaved) indicates the resulting new allegiance. Freedom from sin and slavery to righteousness are the two sides of the same liberation that Christ's atoning death accomplished.
6.19: A theme: Paul uses human analogies about slavery "because of your human limitations." The apology for the metaphor recognizes its limitations; what he is describing (the complete reorientation of the will through Christ's work) is larger than any human analogy can contain. The direction is clear: toward holiness, which is the goal of the union with Christ Paul has been describing.
6.20: A theme: "When you were slaves to sin, you were free from the control of righteousness." The previous freedom from righteousness is exposed as slavery; what looked like liberty was servitude. Christ's liberation reverses this: freedom from sin produces willing slavery to the one whose service is perfect freedom.
6.21: A theme: "What benefit did you reap at that time from the things you are now ashamed of? Those things result in death!" The shame and death produced by the former life contrast with the holiness and eternal life of the new. The question exposes the bankruptcy of the old life by its own outcome — which is the argument by consequences that Paul uses to ground the new life in Christ.
6.22: A direct: "But now that you have been set free from sin and have become slaves of God, the benefit you reap leads to holiness, and the result is eternal life." The Christological logic: freedom from sin (Christ's atoning work) produces willing service to God (the Spirit's work) that leads to holiness (progressive) and eternal life (eschatological). The whole is Christ's doing, not the believer's achievement.
6.23: A direct: "For the wages of sin is death, but the gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord." The final verse of the chapter draws the absolute contrast. Sin operates on wages-logic: it pays out what is earned. God operates on gift-logic: eternal life is given, not earned. The gift is specifically located "in Christ Jesus our Lord" — not as a general divine benevolence but as the fruit of the specific person and work of the Lord Jesus.

7.1: A theme: "Do you not know, brothers and sisters — for I am speaking to those who know the law — that the law has authority over someone only as long as that person lives?" Paul introduces the death-as-release principle using the analogy of law's temporal domain. The principle prepares for the decisive Christological statement of v.4: death with Christ releases from the law's claim.
7.2: A theme: The marriage-law analogy: a married woman is bound by law to her husband as long as he lives; his death frees her. The analogy will be applied in v.4, where the believer's death with Christ is what frees from the law. The point is the legitimate dissolution of one covenant bond by death, enabling a new union.
7.3: A theme: The adultery analogy: if she joins another man while her husband lives, she is an adulteress; if he dies, she is free to remarry without that designation. This establishes that the transition from law to grace is not infidelity to God but the legitimate consequence of death with Christ — the old covenant's claim ended through the believer's death in Christ, not through divorce.
7.4: A direct: "So, my brothers and sisters, you also died to the law through the body of Christ, that you might belong to another, to him who was raised from the dead, in order that we might bear fruit for God." The verse is the application of the analogy. "Through the body of Christ" specifies the mechanism: Christ's physical death is the death through which believers die to the law. The goal is belonging to Christ (marriage analogy completed) and bearing fruit — covenant life in the new union.
7.5: A theme: "For when we were in the realm of the flesh, the sinful passions aroused by the law were at work in us, so that we bore fruit for death." The contrast with v.4's fruit-for-God: the pre-Christ life produced fruit-for-death. The law paradoxically aroused sin rather than suppressing it (amplified in vv.7-25), which is why the solution requires Christ's work, not law-improvement.
7.6: A direct: "But now, by dying to what once bound us, we have been released from the law so that we serve in the new way of the Spirit, and not in the old way of the written code." The Spirit-versus-letter antithesis (cf. 2 Cor 3:6) frames the new covenant's character: not external compliance with a written code but the Spirit's inward renewal, which is the Jeremiah 31 promise now realized in Christ's gift of the Spirit (cf. 8:2).
7.7: A theme: "What shall we say, then? Is the law sinful? Certainly not! Nevertheless, I would not have known what sin was had it not been for the law." The law is declared holy (v.12) and its diagnostic function affirmed. The law's revelation of sin is not a defect but a feature; it is the mirror that shows the condition requiring the physician. Christ does not come despite the law but because of what the law reveals.
7.8: A theme: "But sin, seizing the opportunity afforded by the commandment, produced in me every kind of coveting. For apart from the law, sin was dead." The perverse dynamic: the prohibition activates the desire it prohibits. This is not the law's fault but sin's exploitation of the law's good function. It establishes the impossibility of self-improvement through law-intensification — only death-and-resurrection (ch.6) can break the cycle.
7.9: A theme: "Once I was alive apart from the law; but when the commandment came, sin sprang to life and I died." The narrative of pre-law innocence, law-arrival, sin-activation, and death is the individual analogue of the Adam narrative (cf. 5:12-14). The same pattern: innocence, commandment, transgression, death. Christ's reversal of Adam's path is the only resolution to this structural repetition.
7.10: A theme: "I found that the very commandment that was intended to bring life actually brought death." The commandment's double face: designed for life (Lev 18:5), producing death through sin's exploitation. This is why the law cannot be the gospel — not because it is bad but because it is unable to deal with the sin that subverts it. Only a power stronger than sin's exploitation of the law can bring the life the law promised.
7.11: A theme: "For sin, seizing the opportunity afforded by the commandment, deceived me, and through the commandment put me to death." The language of deception echoes Gen 3:13 (the serpent's deception); sin plays the serpent's role, using the commandment as the occasion for its deceptive work. The pattern from Eden repeats in every human encounter with the law.
7.12: A revelation of God: "So then, the law is holy, and the commandment is holy, righteous and good." Paul's vindication of the Torah: its holiness, righteousness, and goodness are non-negotiable attributes. The law's failure to produce life is not a defect in the law but a disclosure of the depth of the sin-problem. God's law reflects God's holy character; Christ does not abolish but fulfills it (8:4).
7.13: A theme: "Did that which is good, then, become death to me? By no means! Nevertheless, in order that sin might be recognized as sin, it used what is good to bring about my death, so that through the commandment sin might become utterly sinful." The law's revealing function: it makes sin visibly "utterly sinful" by the contrast between the holy commandment and sin's willful transgression of it. This revelation of sin's depth points toward the necessity of an atoning solution rather than a corrective one.
7.14: A theme: "We know that the law is spiritual; but I am unspiritual, sold as a slave to sin." The contrast: law-as-spiritual vs. self-as-unspiritual. The incompatibility between the spiritual law and the unspiritual self is the human predicament that neither willpower nor law-intensification can resolve. The only resolution is the Spirit who fulfills the law's righteous requirement in believers (8:4).
7.15: A theme: "I do not understand what I do. For what I want to do I do not do, but what I hate I do." The conflict described in vv.15-23 reveals the profound disintegration of the will under sin's dominion. Whatever the "I" refers to (regenerate or unregenerate experience is debated), the predicament discloses the impossibility of self-redemption: the will itself is split, wanting the good it cannot achieve.
7.16: A revelation of God: "And if I do what I do not want to do, I agree that the law is good." The inner conflict itself witnesses to the law's goodness: the part of the person that wants to do the right thing is confirming the law's justice. The law's goodness is not nullified by the inability to keep it; that inability drives toward Christ.
7.17: A theme: "As it is, it is no longer I myself who do it, but it is sin living in me." The distinction between the person and the sin-power dwelling in them is not an escape from responsibility but a diagnostic: sin is a power that has taken up residence in the person, not merely a list of bad choices. What is needed is an exorcism of sin from its habitation, which only Christ's death and the Spirit's indwelling can accomplish.
7.18: A theme: "For I know that good itself does not dwell in me, that is, in my sinful nature. For I have the desire to do what is good, but I cannot carry it out." The limits of good intentions without transformative power: the desire is present, the capacity absent. This is the gap that Christ's work bridges: not by strengthening the will but by dealing with the sin that makes the will ineffective (6:6-7; 8:3-4).
7.19: A theme: "For I do not do the good I want to do, but the evil I do not want to do — this I keep on doing." The repetition of the same conflict (cf. v.15) emphasizes the persistence of the predicament and the inability of insight or intention to resolve it. The resolution must come from outside the self entirely.
7.20: A theme: "Now if I do what I do not want to do, it is no longer I who do it, but it is sin living in me that does it." The repeated formulation underlines the alien-power quality of sin; the person who wants the good is not the same as the agent producing the evil, though they are the same person. This is the depth of sin's colonization of the human person that makes rescue rather than reform the necessary response.
7.21: A theme: "So I find this law at work: Although I want to do good, evil is right there with me." The "law" here is not the Mosaic law but the empirical principle of habitual evil co-present with the intention to do good. The structural entanglement of good intention and evil performance is the natural condition that Christ's atoning work addresses at the level of co-crucifixion with the old self (6:6).
7.22: A revelation of God: "For in my inner being I delight in God's law." Whatever the identity of the "I" in this chapter, the delight in God's law in the inner person is significant: the law itself is not the enemy. The conflict is between the person who wants to honor the law and the sin-power that prevents it. Christ fulfills the law's righteous requirement (8:4) precisely because no fallen person can do so from within the old Adam.
7.23: A theme: "But I see another law at work in me, waging war against the law of my mind and making me a prisoner of the law of sin at work within me." The warfare metaphor: sin wages active campaign against the renewed mind's intention. The captivity Paul describes is the condition from which Christ's rescue (v.24-25) delivers; it is not the permanent condition of the believer but the vivid description of what awaits without him.
7.24: A direct: "What a wretched man I am! Who will rescue me from this body that is subject to death?" The cry for rescue (rysesthai = deliver, rescue) is addressed to a liberator outside the self; it cannot be self-rescue. The question "who will?" anticipates the answer in v.25, which names the deliverer rather than a program or technique. The cry establishes the soteriological logic: the problem is one that only a person can resolve, which is why the gospel is centered on a person.
7.25: A direct: "Thanks be to God, who delivers me through Jesus Christ our Lord!" The exclamation of rescue answers the cry of v.24. The deliverer is named: Jesus Christ our Lord. The Christological specificity is the answer to the existential cry — not a principle, not a technique, not a moral program, but a person through whom God delivers the one who cries out. The mediation is explicit: "through Jesus Christ," not through human effort reshaped by Christ's teaching, but through the person of the mediator himself.

8.1: A direct: "There is now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus." The verse states the Christological conclusion of the entire argument from 1:18 through 7:25: the condemnation that sin deserved and that the law pronounced has been removed by Christ's atoning work (3:24-26; 8:3). The location "in Christ Jesus" is the sphere in which the verdict stands.
8.2: A direct: The law of the Spirit of life "in Christ Jesus" has set free from the law of sin and death. The liberation is specifically located in union with Christ; the Spirit who liberates is the "Spirit of Christ" (v.9) — the Spirit given because Christ's atoning work opened the way for the Spirit's full indwelling of God's people (cf. Ezek 36:26-27 fulfilled through Christ).
8.3: A direct: "God did this by sending his own Son in the likeness of sinful flesh and as a sin offering, and so he condemned sin in the flesh." The two movements — sending the Son and condemning sin through him — express the Incarnation and the Atonement as a single divine act. The "sin offering" language (peri hamartias) places Christ's death in continuity with the Levitical system it fulfills and surpasses.
8.4: A direct: The Spirit enables "the righteous requirement of the law" to be "fully met in us." The law's demand — what no human could satisfy — is satisfied in Christ (who kept it perfectly) and is fulfilled in those who are in Christ through the Spirit's enabling work. Christ's fulfilment of the law is not its abolition but its eschatological completion.
8.5: A theme: The two orientations (flesh and Spirit) correspond to two ways of living that lead to different ends. Christ's death and resurrection established the new orientation as possible; the Spirit's presence is the marker of those who live according to the Spirit. The two-ways theme runs from Ps 1 through Deut 30 to here, now defined Christologically.
8.6: A theme: The mind governed by the Spirit brings life and peace. The "life and peace" that the Spirit-orientation produces is the eschatological shalom that Christ's resurrection secured (5:1 peace with God; 8:11 bodily resurrection). The Spirit-governed life is the present experience of what Christ accomplished.
8.7: A revelation of God: The flesh is "hostile to God" and "does not submit to God's law." The depth of the enmity that Christ was sent to overcome (5:10 "we were God's enemies... reconciled through the death of his Son") is here named in its full character: structural, constitutional hostility. The magnitude of the Incarnation and Atonement is commensurate with the depth of this enmity.
8.8: A theme: Those in the realm of the flesh "cannot please God." The impossibility of pleasing God from within the flesh-domain grounds the necessity of the Spirit's work, which itself is made possible by Christ's atonement. The law's failure (8:3) and the flesh's inability (8:8) converge to establish why "sending the Son" was the only solution.
8.9: A direct: "You... are not in the realm of the flesh but are in the realm of the Spirit, if indeed the Spirit of God lives in you. And if anyone does not have the Spirit of Christ, they do not belong to Christ." The equation of "Spirit of God" and "Spirit of Christ" is a high Christological claim: the Spirit who is God's is equally Christ's. Belonging to Christ is defined by Spirit-possession.
